patches.description: During solar minimum the speed distribution splits into two peaks: a slow-wind peak at 355 km/s and a fast-wind shoulder at 622 km/s. This bimodality motivates the saturation framework built on later figures.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (355 and 622 km/s peaks); paper abstract and Section 1 (bimodality during solar minima motivates two-state paradigm); figure-detail voice — declarative finding, concrete numbers lead.

patches.description: Helium abundance is set in the chromosphere below the sonic point; cross helicity is set between the sonic and Alfven surfaces. The two diagnostics fingerprint distinct heights of the Sun-heliosphere coupling chain.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper Figure 2 caption (A_He set below r_c, |sigma_c| set between r_c and r_A); calibration spec § Worked Example 1 'set at X, fingerprints Y' parallel structure.

patches.description: Helium abundance saturates at 4.19% above 433 km/s. A bilinear fit to A_He versus solar wind speed defines the saturation point (v_s, A_s) that anchors the saturation framework.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper Section 3.1 (A_s = 4.19% +/- 0.05%, v_s = 433 +/- 4.0 km/s); figure-detail voice — declarative finding with the foundational number.

patches.description: Fast wind sits almost exclusively at high cross helicity (|sigma_c| approaching 1) while slow wind spans the full 0-to-1 range. Wave activity has no preferred level in slow wind but is uniformly high above v_s.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper Section 1 (slow wind does not display preferred |sigma_c|, fast wind |sigma_c| approaches 1); figure-detail voice.

patches.description: The saturation speed shifts systematically with wave activity. Across 15 cross-helicity quantiles, higher |sigma_c| pulls v_s to lower speeds — the first evidence that wave-driven acceleration tilts the helium transition.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field ('shifts systematically'); paper Section 3 fit in 15 |sigma_c| quantiles; alt-text describing 15 curves colored by |sigma_c|.

patches.description: Rescaling each cross-helicity bin to its own (v_s, A_s) collapses all 15 curves onto a single trajectory below saturation. Helium depletion in slow wind follows one universal process independent of wave activity.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; technical_caption (curves rescaled to (1,1)); alt-text (collapse onto universal curve).

patches.description: As cross helicity climbs, the saturation speed drops 23 km/s (433 to 410) while the saturation abundance rises 0.26 percentage points (3.87% to 4.13%). The anticorrelation locates the Alfvenic slow wind in the A_He-|sigma_c| plane.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (exact 23 km/s and 0.26 pp shifts); paper abstract (v_s decreases as Alfvenicity increases while A_s increases); diagnostic-split framing per spec § Claim Grounding.

patches.description: Helium density peaks at 409 km/s — at the boundary between magnetically closed and open source regions — while hydrogen density falls monotonically with speed. The helium peak locates the source-region transition.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (409 km/s peak; closed-to-open transition); paper Section discussing source-region transition speeds; figure-detail declarative voice.

patches.description: Five Table 2 speeds partition the solar-minimum wind distribution into source-region segments: the slow and fast peaks, the helium-density peak v_n ≈ 409 km/s, the helium-saturation speed v_s ≈ 430 km/s, and their Gaussian intersection v_i = 484 km/s.
patches.voice_grounding_source: Table 2 + Figure 9 caption (paper.md ll. 180-182); five speeds v_slow, v_n, v_s, v_i, v_fast; quantitative emphasis.
patches.audit_changes: User-resolved L10/L14 flag (2026-05-13): 'vanishing speed' (v_v from Table 1 / Kasper 2007) is not one of Figure 9's highlighted speeds. Rewrote to reference Table 2 speeds actually shown: v_slow, v_n, v_s, v_i, v_fast.

patches.description: Mean speed contoured over the A_He-|sigma_c| plane separates two populations: low-helium / low-|sigma_c| closed-source wind and high-helium / high-|sigma_c| open-source wind. The diagnostic plane sorts sources without composition data.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper abstract (novel classification scheme using A_He and |sigma_c|); diagnostic-split framing per spec § Claim Grounding.

patches.description: The 10th-percentile speed stays below 390 km/s everywhere in the A_He-|sigma_c| plane, including its open-source corner. Some open-field plasma reaches Earth at canonically slow speeds — the Alfvenic slow wind population.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (360-390 km/s slowest 10% everywhere); paper inference about Alfvenic slow wind from open sources at slow speeds.

patches.description: The 90th-percentile speed exceeds 420 km/s across the closed-field portion of the plane, ranging up to 510 km/s. Closed-source plasma can reach speeds traditionally classified as fast wind.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (90th percentile 420-510 km/s in closed-field region); paper Section on closed-source fast wind.

patches.description: Four regions partition the A_He-|sigma_c| plane by source magnetic topology — closed, open, transitional, and Alfvenic slow. The classification maps in-situ observations to source regions without composition or charge-state data.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper abstract (novel solar wind categorization scheme); diagnostic-split framing per spec § Claim Grounding.

patches.description: Helium abundance rises with speed up to 402 km/s, then saturates at roughly half its photospheric value. ACE/SWICS reproduces the Wind-derived saturation framework, validating the bilinear fit across instruments.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper Section 3.1 (v_s = 402 +/- 2 km/s, A_s = 0.530 photospheric); paper Section 2 (ACE/SWICS comparison to Wind/SWE).

patches.description: All heavy elements (C, N, O, Ne, Mg, Si, S, Fe) saturate near 327 km/s — 75 km/s slower than helium at 402 km/s. The fast-slow transition speed for heavy ions sits well below the helium transition.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (75 km/s slower); paper Section 3.1 (heavy-ion mean v_s = 327 +/- 2 km/s, He v_s = 402 km/s); paper abstract Result (1).

patches.description: Below the transition speed, all heavy elements collapse onto a single gradient once scaled to their own saturation points. Slow wind shows no element-specific fractionation — a universal compositional process operates below v_s.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; technical_caption (curves scaled to (v_s, A_s) at (1,1)); paper Section 3.1 conclusion on universal slow-wind gradient.

patches.description: Heavy-ion transition speeds are independent of first ionization potential. The fast-slow transition is set by a process that does not discriminate between low-FIP and high-FIP elements.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper abstract Result (2) (v_s independent of mass and charge state); paper Section noting low-FIP/high-FIP v_s identical within uncertainties.

patches.description: Heavy-ion abundances at the transition speed reproduce the canonical FIP effect: low-FIP elements enhanced roughly twofold over high-FIP elements. The saturation-point abundances match prior fast-wind measurements.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (twofold low-FIP enhancement); paper abstract Result (3) (transition abundances consistent with prior fast-wind observations); paper Section on FIP fractionation.

patches.description: In fast wind, lighter heavy elements are enhanced more above their saturation values than heavier ones, forming a clear mass-dependent trend. A second fractionation process operates only above v_s.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; technical_caption (decreasing trend with mass indicates heavy-ion fractionation in fast wind); paper abstract Result (4).

patches.description: The heavy-ion transition at 327 km/s sits just below the most common solar wind speed; helium's transition at 402 km/s sits just above it. Both characteristic speeds bracket the peak of the wind speed distribution.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field; paper Section 3.1 v_s values for heavy ions and helium; alt-text describing histogram with both transitions marked.

patches.description: Fast-wind heavy-ion fractionation organizes by charge state with R-squared 0.95, versus below 0.55 for mass, FIP, and mass-to-charge ratio. Charge state — not mass — drives the yet-to-be-determined fractionation mechanism above v_s.
patches.voice_grounding_source: summary_short field (R^2 = 0.95 versus <0.55); paper abstract Conclusion (3) (heavy ions fractionated in fast wind by yet-to-be-determined mechanism); paper Section on charge-state organization.
